title: Topic 176: Why Players Defend Bad Games: Brand Loyalty and Identity
content:
# Topic 176: Why Players Defend Bad Games: Brand Loyalty and Identity

## Abstract

This paper delves into the fascinating, often bewildering world of video game enthusiasts who staunchly defend objectively flawed games. It is a journey into the heart of digital cognitive dissonance, where bugs are features, and a multi-million-dollar corporation is a dear friend in need of protection. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, examining how brand loyalty, effort justification, and the sunk cost fallacy conspire to turn a discerning consumer into a valiant apologist. Through a blend of academic theory, real-world case studies, and a healthy dose of incredulity, we seek to understand why a player who has spent one hundred hours clipping through the floor in a buggy mess will declare it a masterpiece. Ultimately, this research serves as a testament to the human mind's extraordinary ability to justify its choices, proving that loyalty, much like a Bethesda game, knows no logical bounds.

## Introduction

Cast your mind back to the winter of 2020. The air was thick with anticipation and, as it would turn out, a litany of game-breaking bugs. *Cyberpunk 2077*, a title hyped into the stratosphere, was finally released. The result was less of a glorious technological revolution and more of a public service announcement on the dangers of pre-ordering. Cars fell from the sky, characters assumed terrifying T-poses, and the city of Night City was seemingly held together by digital duct tape. And yet, amidst the roaring flames of criticism and the great console return debacle, a peculiar sound could be heard: the steadfast murmur of defense. “It’s not that bad,” some said. “You just need a better PC,” insisted others. “The story is a work of art!” they cried, while their character’s pants mysteriously vanished.

This curious case of digital apologism is not an isolated incident. It is a recurring theme in the annals of gaming history, a testament to a loyalty that defies logic, reason, and sometimes, basic functionality. Why do we, as players, feel compelled to defend the games we love, even when they are demonstrably, unequivocally, broken? This paper will argue that this behavior is not simply a matter of stubbornness, but a complex psychological cocktail of brand loyalty, cognitive dissonance, and the powerful influence of community identity. We will explore how a simple leisure activity can become so intertwined with our sense of self that a critique of a game feels like a personal affront. By dissecting the mental gymnastics required to defend the indefensible, we can gain a deeper understanding of not just gamer psychology, but the very nature of modern consumerism. So, grab your +5 Sword of Confirmation Bias, and let us venture forth into the often-irrational world of player devotion.

## Literature Review

To understand the modern gamer’s unwavering defense of a beloved, albeit broken, digital world, one must first consult the ancient scrolls of consumer psychology. The phenomenon is not born from a vacuum but is a potent manifestation of long-established theories, beginning with the powerful concept of **brand loyalty**. Fournier (1998) posited that consumers do not simply purchase products; they form relationships with brands, treating them with the same complexity and emotional investment as human connections [1]. In the gaming world, this translates to a fierce allegiance not just to a single game, but to the developers and publishers behind it. This “brand relationship” can become so ingrained that, as Palomba (2016) observed in the context of video game consoles, consumers may actively “eschew information that runs counter to their beliefs” and even “vilify other rival brands” [2]. The infamous “console wars” serve as a perennial, and often comical, real-world example of this tribalistic brand devotion in action.

However, brand loyalty alone does not explain the defense of a *bad* product. For that, we must turn to the psychological discomfort known as **cognitive dissonance**. First proposed by Leon Festinger in the 1950s, cognitive dissonance describes the mental stress experienced when holding two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values, or when one's beliefs are inconsistent with one's actions. In gaming, this manifests when a player has invested significant time, money, and emotional energy into a game (Action) only to discover that the game is riddled with flaws (Belief). To resolve this dissonance, the player must either admit their investment was a mistake or, more commonly, perform a series of mental acrobatics to justify their continued engagement. This leads directly to the principle of **effort justification**, a close cousin of cognitive dissonance, which suggests that the more effort we expend on an activity, the more we value its outcome. The player who has spent a hundred hours grinding for loot in a buggy RPG is psychologically primed to view that experience as profoundly meaningful, lest they admit to themselves that they have wasted a significant portion of their finite existence.

Finally, we must consider the **sunk cost fallacy**, the human tendency to continue an endeavor because of previously invested resources, rather than on the basis of future prospects. In gaming, this is the force that compels a player to log back into a massively multiplayer online game they no longer enjoy, simply because they have already poured hundreds of dollars into virtual hats and subscriptions. The money is gone, the time is spent, but to stop playing would be to acknowledge the loss. It is far easier, psychologically, to convince oneself that the fun is just around the corner, that the next expansion will fix everything, and that the investment will, eventually, pay off. Together, these three pillars—brand loyalty, cognitive dissonance, and the sunk cost fallacy—form the holy trinity of gamer apologism, a powerful psychological force that can turn even the most discerning critic into a loyal defender of the digital realm.

## Analysis

Having established the theoretical framework, we can now construct a more detailed, and admittedly satirical, portrait of the psychological journey from player to staunch defender—a process we shall call “The Five Stages of Fanboyism.” This model illustrates how the confluence of brand loyalty, cognitive dissonance, and community reinforcement can transform a rational consumer into a digital crusader, armed with a keyboard and an unshakeable conviction that their favorite, flawed game is, in fact, a misunderstood masterpiece.

**Stage 1: The Honeymoon and the Investment.** The journey begins with excitement. A player, lured by a slick marketing campaign and a developer’s sterling reputation, invests in a new game. This investment is not merely financial; it is also temporal and emotional. They pour hours into the game, learn its systems, and begin to forge an emotional connection with its world and characters. This initial period is often characterized by a genuine enjoyment and a willingness to overlook minor imperfections. The brand relationship is in its nascent, blissful stage.

**Stage 2: The Cracks Appear and Dissonance Sets In.** Inevitably, the game’s more significant flaws begin to surface. A game-breaking bug halts progress, the story takes a nonsensical turn, or the monetization model reveals its predatory nature. This is the critical juncture where cognitive dissonance emerges. The player is now faced with two conflicting realities: “I have invested heavily in this game and identify as a fan,” and “This game is, in many respects, quite bad.” The psychological tension is palpable. To admit the latter would be to invalidate the former, a deeply uncomfortable proposition.

**Stage 3: The Justification Engine Roars to Life.** To resolve this dissonance, the player’s mind engages in a remarkable feat of self-deception. This is where effort justification and the sunk cost fallacy take the wheel. The player begins to rationalize. “The bugs are actually kind of funny,” they might tell themselves. “The story is deep; you just don’t get it.” They start to magnify the game’s positive aspects while minimizing its negatives. The hundred hours of tedious grinding were not a waste; they were a testament to the game’s “depth” and “challenge.” The money spent on microtransactions was not a loss; it was a way to “support the developers.”

**Stage 4: The Echo Chamber and Identity Fusion.** Seeking validation, the player gravitates towards online communities that share their newfound perspective. They join the game’s official subreddit, Discord server, or fan forums. Here, they find a chorus of like-minded individuals, all engaged in the same process of collective justification. This is the echo chamber, a space where positive opinions are amplified, and negative ones are dismissed as the ravings of “haters” or “trolls.” Within this digital sanctuary, the player’s identity begins to fuse with the game. It is no longer just a product they enjoy; it is a part of who they are. An attack on the game is now perceived as a personal attack, triggering a defensive, often aggressive, response.

**Stage 5: The Crusader.** The transformation is complete. The player is no longer a mere consumer but a self-appointed guardian of the game’s reputation. They patrol forums, downvote critical comments, and engage in lengthy, passionate arguments with detractors. They have fully internalized the brand’s narrative, often defending the company’s most anti-consumer practices with a fervor that would make a marketing executive weep with joy. They have become a living, breathing embodiment of brand loyalty, their initial enjoyment of a game having evolved into a deeply entrenched, psychologically fortified position. The game may be bad, but their defense of it is, in their mind, a noble and necessary cause.

## Real-World Examples

The theoretical journey of our “fanboy” is not a mere academic exercise; it is a narrative that plays out time and again in the real world. The gaming landscape is littered with case studies of flawed launches and the unyielding communities that rallied around them, providing a fertile ground for observing these psychological principles in their natural habitat.

**Case Study 1: The *Cyberpunk 2077* Redemption Arc.** As mentioned in the introduction, the launch of *Cyberpunk 2077* was a technical cataclysm. Yet, beneath the avalanche of criticism, a dedicated community of defenders emerged. These players, often running the game on high-end PCs that mitigated the worst of the bugs, became its most vocal champions. They pointed to the game’s undeniable artistic achievements—the stunning world design, the compelling narrative, the memorable characters—as evidence of its inherent quality. The technical issues, they argued, were temporary, a mere blemish on a work of genius. This was a classic case of cognitive dissonance resolution; by focusing on the game’s strengths and framing its weaknesses as transient, they could justify their continued investment. Their faith was, in a sense, rewarded. Developer CD Projekt Red embarked on a multi-year campaign of patches and updates, culminating in the critically acclaimed *Phantom Liberty* expansion. The game’s eventual redemption served as the ultimate validation for its early defenders, reinforcing the narrative that their loyalty was not misplaced, but prescient.

**Case Study 2: The Wasteland Tenacity of *Fallout 76*.** If *Cyberpunk 2077* was a story of a rocky launch, *Fallout 76* was a full-blown nuclear apocalypse. The game was released in a state that was, by most accounts, unfinished. It was buggy, lacked key features, and was plagued by a series of PR disasters. The backlash was immense. And yet, a community of players not only endured but thrived. They embraced the game’s desolate, often broken, world and began to build their own societies within it. They organized in-game events, established player-run factions, and created a culture of mutual support. For this community, the game became a backdrop for their shared experiences. Their loyalty was less to the product itself and more to the community they had built. This fostered a powerful “us vs. the world” mentality, a form of collective identity fusion where criticism of the game was seen as an attack on their hard-won digital home.

**Case Study 3: The Unbreakable Charm of Bethesda Game Studios.** No discussion of defending flawed games would be complete without mentioning Bethesda, the undisputed masters of the buggy-yet-beloved epic. From *Morrowind* to *Skyrim* to *Starfield*, Bethesda’s games are renowned for their vast, immersive worlds and their equally vast collection of bizarre and often hilarious glitches. The company’s fanbase has, over the years, developed a unique and endearing relationship with these imperfections. The common refrain, “It’s not a bug, it’s a feature,” has become a mantra, a humorous acknowledgment of the developer’s consistent shortcomings. This is perhaps the most sophisticated form of effort justification, one that has evolved into a shared cultural in-joke. Furthermore, the vibrant modding community that surrounds Bethesda’s games plays a crucial role in sustaining this loyalty. Players know that even if the game is broken, the community will, in time, fix it. This allows them to purchase and defend the games with a kind of preemptive faith, secure in the knowledge that their investment will, with a little help from their friends, eventually be worthwhile.

## Discussion

The curious case of the defended bad game offers a fascinating window into the modern consumer psyche. This behavior, while often baffling to outsiders, reveals a great deal about our innate need for consistency, community, and the justification of our own choices. The psychological mechanisms at play are not unique to gaming; they are universal. The Apple enthusiast who dismisses every criticism of the latest iPhone, the sports fan who defends their perpetually losing team, and the political partisan who ignores the failings of their chosen candidate are all engaging in a similar form of psychological self-preservation. The gaming world, with its passionate communities and significant investments of time and money, simply provides a particularly potent and observable arena for these behaviors to manifest.

For the gaming industry, this phenomenon is a double-edged sword. On one hand, this fierce brand loyalty can be a powerful asset, creating a resilient customer base that is willing to forgive the occasional misstep. On the other hand, it can foster a culture of complacency, where publishers feel empowered to release unfinished or unpolished products, confident that a core of dedicated fans will weather the initial storm of criticism. The line between a loyal fan and an apologist for anti-consumer practices can become dangerously blurred. The challenge for the discerning consumer is to remain a passionate supporter without becoming an enabler of bad behavior.

Ultimately, the defense of a bad game is a testament to the subjective and often irrational nature of fun. It is a reminder that our enjoyment of a piece of media is not solely determined by its objective quality, but by a complex interplay of personal investment, social context, and psychological need. We find joy not just in the polished perfection of a masterpiece, but in the shared struggle of overcoming a buggy mess, in the camaraderie of a community built on a foundation of shared digital hardship. In a strange way, the defense of a bad game is a celebration of the human ability to find meaning and connection in the most unexpected, and often broken, of places.

## Conclusion

In the final analysis, the act of defending a bad game is a profoundly human one. It is a behavior born not of ignorance or stubbornness, but of a complex and deeply ingrained set of psychological imperatives. The potent cocktail of brand loyalty, the discomfort of cognitive dissonance, the relentless pull of the sunk cost fallacy, and the warm embrace of identity-fused communities all conspire to turn a simple consumer into a valiant digital knight, defending their chosen kingdom against all detractors. We invest our time, our money, and a piece of ourselves into these virtual worlds, and it is only natural that we would fight to protect that investment.

This phenomenon serves as a humorous, yet insightful, microcosm of broader human behavior, reflecting our universal need to find meaning in our choices and solidarity in our passions. So, the next time you see a player passionately defending a game that appears to be a glorious train wreck, do not be too quick to judge. You are not witnessing a moment of madness, but rather a beautiful, baffling, and deeply human spectacle: the mind’s desperate, and often hilarious, attempt to prove that it was right all along, even when the game itself is telling it otherwise.
references:
[1] Fournier, S. (1998). Consumers and Their Brands: Developing Relationship Theory in Consumer Research. *Journal of Consumer Research*, 24(4), 343–373.

[2] Palomba, A. (2016). The Antecedents of Interactive Loyalty. *Online Journal of Communication and Media Technologies*, 6(2), 107-128.

[3] Festinger, L. (1957). *A Theory of Cognitive Dissonance*. Stanford University Press.

[4] Reddit Community. (2021). *Why do people actively defend bad actors in the gaming industry?* [Online forum post]. Retrieved from https://www.reddit.com/r/truegaming/comments/lzj3i3/why_do_people_actively_defend_bad_actors_in_the/
